summary: Market sentiment is cautiously optimistic following the S&P 500 and Nasdaq posting their best quarter since 2020, though gains were achieved against a backdrop of significant geopolitical turbulence driven by the Iran conflict and an AI-driven rally that created a volatile, bifurcated market environment. The Iran war remains the dominant macro force, with Tehran's refusal to meet U.S. envoys eliminating near-term prospects for a peace deal, while Israel's continued presence in southern Lebanon and ongoing regional instability keep geopolitical risk premiums elevated. At the sector level, the AI boom propelled chipmakers to their best quarter on record, though Nvidia notably underperformed its peers, raising questions about whether its valuation and product cycle can reignite leadership, while on Wall Street, analysts are rotating out of Goldman Sachs in favor of Capital One, signaling a preference for consumer-facing financials over investment banking. Key risks investors are monitoring include escalating Middle East tensions threatening oil supply chains — underscored by the UAE exporting record crude volumes following its OPEC exit and rising Asian import uncertainty — alongside potential demand destruction if geopolitical shocks intensify. In the short term, markets face a tug-of-war between sustained AI-driven momentum and geopolitical headwinds, with traders likely to remain reactive to any developments in Iran-U.S. diplomacy and further signals on whether Nvidia and the broader chip sector can maintain their historic pace of gains.
